assertions: Defines a TypeLambda brand for Tree (an interface extending TypeLambda with `type: Tree<this["Target"]>`)
Imports Kind/TypeLambda from effect/HKT and typeclass interfaces from @effect/typeclass
Provides a Covariant instance parameterised by the Tree brand
The map implementation recurses correctly into the children array
Avoids invalid `F<A>` HKT syntax; applies the type constructor via Kind
evals.expected_output: A TreeTypeLambda brand, a Covariant (and ideally Foldable/Traversable) instance for Tree, correct use of Kind, recursion handled. The core workflow done right.
evals.name: effect-repo-tree-instance
evals.probes: Core-workflow correctness: SKILL.md Steps 2-4 and the templates.
evals.prompt: I've got a `Tree` type in my Effect project — `type Tree<A> = { value: A; children: ReadonlyArray<Tree<A>> }` — and I want it to work with `@effect/typeclass` so I can map over it generically and reuse Effect's combinators. Can you set up the typeclass instances?

assertions: Names the latent abstraction (Option/Maybe with a Functor/map) behind the repeated ...OrNull patterns
Weighs the cost of going against the team's imperative/class-based style
Addresses the decision of adding the effect dependency to a non-FP repo
Gives an explicit, reasoned recommendation rather than only listing options
Does not jump straight to a full HKT encoding without justifying that it is warranted
evals.expected_output: Recognizes the latent Option/Functor opportunity, weighs the against-the-grain cost honestly (team consistency, the new `effect` dependency, onboarding), and gives a clear recommendation rather than reflexively encoding HKTs.
evals.name: nonfp-repo-recognize-opportunity
evals.probes: Whether the skill can SPOT an opportunity and reason about adopting a typeclass in a non-FP repo. Most likely to reveal a missing reference.
evals.prompt: Our codebase is a plain TypeScript Node API — no FP libraries, mostly classes and imperative code. We've got six different `...OrNull` patterns and manual null-checking all over the place. I have a hunch some kind of typeclass abstraction could clean this up, but I don't know if it's worth fighting the team's style. Thoughts?

assertions: Identifies Applicative (or SemiApplicative) as the typeclass for accumulating all errors
Explains why monadic sequencing short-circuits while applicative composition accumulates
Points to the specific @effect/typeclass module(s) to use
Explains the terms (applicative, monad) without assuming the user already knows them
evals.expected_output: Routes the behavior (accumulate all errors) to Applicative / SemiApplicative, explains why a monad short-circuits and an applicative accumulates, and points at the right @effect/typeclass module.
evals.name: behavior-without-vocabulary
evals.probes: Whether the catalog lets the skill map a described behavior to the correct typeclass.
evals.prompt: I've got a `Validated<E, A>` type in our Effect codebase and a form with 8 fields. Right now validation bails on the first error, but product wants every error shown at once. A teammate said something about needing an 'applicative' instead of a monad? I genuinely don't know what that means or which `@effect/typeclass` thing I'm supposed to use. Can you sort this out?

assertions: Explains in plain terms what 'typeclass conventions' / @effect/typeclass means
Identifies RemoteData as a two-type-parameter type and maps E and A to the correct Kind slots
Gives concrete, ordered first steps (define the brand, then the instances)
Does not assume prior FP or HKT knowledge
evals.expected_output: Gives a lost, ticket-driven user an unambiguous path: explains what 'typeclass conventions' likely means, identifies RemoteData as a two-parameter type, walks the brand + instances, without assuming prior FP knowledge.
evals.name: ticket-driven-lost-user
evals.probes: Whether a no-background user gets a clear on-ramp; reveals whether SKILL.md needs a gentler entry or a glossary.
evals.prompt: Got handed a ticket: 'Make `RemoteData` conform to our typeclass conventions (@effect/typeclass).' That's the whole ticket. `RemoteData` is `type RemoteData<E, A> = { _tag: 'NotAsked' } | { _tag: 'Loading' } | { _tag: 'Failure'; error: E } | { _tag: 'Success'; value: A }`. I've never touched this part of the codebase and I don't know what 'typeclass conventions' even means here. Where do I start?

assertions: Applies a when-to-use judgment gate instead of immediately complying with the request
Surfaces the standing cost of the HKT encoding (inference annotations, opaque error messages)
Reaches an explicit recommendation: either a justified encoding or the simpler concrete path
evals.expected_output: Applies the Step 0 gate honestly to a borderline case — surfaces the tradeoff rather than just complying, and either justifies the encoding or recommends the simpler concrete path.
evals.name: judgment-gate-borderline
evals.probes: Whether the judgment gate handles a borderline maybe-over-engineering request.
evals.prompt: In my TS app I've got two result-ish types, `ApiResult<E, A>` and `FormState<E, A>`, and I keep duplicating little helpers like `mapValue` and `mapError` for each one. I'm thinking I should make them higher-kinded so I can write the helpers once. Walk me through doing that?

assertions: Explains ts(2315): F has kind * and cannot be applied as F<A>
Gives the fix: constrain F to TypeLambda and apply it via Kind
Shows corrected code that would compile
evals.expected_output: Identifies ts(2315) as the first-order-kind wall, explains why, and shows the fix: constrain F to TypeLambda and apply it with Kind.
evals.name: debug-ts2315
evals.prompt: This won't compile and the error makes no sense to me. I'm trying to write a reusable Functor interface: `interface Functor<F> { map: <A, B>(fa: F<A>, f: (a: A) => B) => F<B> }` — TypeScript says `Type 'F' is not generic`. How do I fix it?
